cues.x: Welcome.
Today we are spending time with one of the most quietly powerful little books ever written about money.
It is called The Richest Man in Babylon, and it was written by George S. Clason back in the 1920s.
If you have never heard of it, that is fine.
If you have, you probably remember one line from it, and we will get to that line, because it might be the single most important sentence you ever hear about building wealth.
Settle in.
Whether you are driving right now, or walking, or lifting something heavy at the gym, I want you to imagine something with me.
I want you to imagine a city made of gold.
Not literally, but close.
A city so rich that kings from every corner of the ancient world sent their treasure there for safekeeping.
That city was Babylon.
And here is the thing that makes this book so strange and so wonderful.
Clason did not write a dry finance manual.
He wrote parables.
Little stories, set thousands of years ago, among chariot builders and merchants and camel traders and slaves and money lenders.
He dressed the lessons up in ancient robes.
And the reason he did that is genius, because it lets you see the truth about money without all the noise of the modern world getting in the way.
No stock tickers.
No apps.
No crypto.
Just people, and gold, and the simple, unbending laws that decide who keeps it and who loses it.
And that is the first big idea I want to plant in your mind before we even meet the characters.
The laws of money have not changed.
Not in four thousand years.
The tools have changed.
The clothes have changed.
The language has changed.
But the actual rules that govern whether wealth flows toward you or away from you, those are as fixed and as reliable as the rising of the sun.
A man in ancient Babylon who saved a portion of what he earned and put it to work grew rich by the exact same mechanism that makes people rich today.
And a man who spent everything he made, no matter how much he made, stayed poor by the exact same mechanism that keeps people poor today.
When you really absorb that, it changes how you listen to every piece of money advice you will ever hear.
You stop chasing the shiny new thing and you start looking for the old, boring, unchanging law underneath it.
So let us go to Babylon.
Let us meet the two men who kick off this whole story, because you are going to recognize yourself in them.
Their names were Bansir and Kobbi.
Bansir was a chariot builder, one of the best in the city.
He worked hard.
He was skilled.
His chariots were known for their quality.
Kobbi was a musician, a lyre player, and he too was gifted at his craft.
These were not lazy men.
These were not fools.
These were men who got up every day and did honest, difficult work with their hands and their talent.
And yet, here is where we find them at the start of the book.
Bansir is sitting on the low wall in front of his house, staring at nothing, doing no work, his purse completely empty.
And his old friend Kobbi comes by to borrow a couple of coins so he can play at a feast that night, and he discovers that Bansir, the master chariot builder, has nothing to lend him.
Both of them, hardworking their whole lives, and both of them broke.
And they sit there together and they ask the question that I think almost everyone listening to this has asked at some point in a quiet moment.
Why?
Why is it that we labor so hard, day after day, year after year, and we have nothing to show for it?
We are skilled.
We are diligent.
And still our purses are thin.
Meanwhile others in the city, men they knew, men who did not seem any smarter or any more hardworking than they were, had grown wealthy.
Bansir says something that stings when you hear it.
He says, in all the years we have worked, we have earned much, yet the joys of wealth are known to us only in our dreams.
Are we but dumb sheep, he asks, driven by others, forever?
He is angry.
He is frustrated.
And he is honest.
Now hold onto that feeling, because that frustration is the beginning of everything.
Most people never even get that far.
They stay numb.
They keep their heads down and they never stop to ask why the money runs out every month.
But Bansir and Kobbi stop.
They question it.
And then they do the smartest thing they could possibly do.
They decide to go and ask someone who actually figured it out.
They say to each other, we have sought wisdom from those who had it not.
We have listened to those who were as poor as ourselves.
So let us go instead to our old friend Arkad, the richest man in all of Babylon, and let us ask him how he did it.
And that decision, that simple decision to learn from someone who has actually achieved the thing you want rather than from your equally broke friends and neighbors, is a lesson all by itself.
Be careful whose advice you take.
We will come back to that hard, because it nearly ruined Arkad himself.
So who was this Arkad?
Here is the part that should give you hope.
Arkad was not born rich.
Arkad started exactly where Bansir and Kobbi were.
He was, in his youth, a humble scribe.
He carved words into clay tablets for long hours, for very little pay.
He came from no great family.
He inherited no fortune.
He had no special luck.
He was, in every way, an ordinary young man of modest means.

And yet he became so wealthy that his name was known throughout the city as the richest man in Babylon, and he was famous also for his generosity, because he gave freely, and still his fortune only grew.
When his old friends come to him and ask him to reveal his secret, Arkad does not brush them off.
He does not pretend it was magic or fate.
He tells them the truth about how it happened, and it started with a night of hard work and a single question.
Arkad tells them about a man named Algamish, a rich money lender.
One day Algamish came to the house of records where Arkad was working and demanded that a certain law be copied onto a clay tablet by the end of the day, or there would be trouble.
It was a huge amount of work.
And young Arkad, seeing an opportunity, made Algamish a deal.
He said, if I finish this entire tablet for you by the end of the day, will you tell me the secret of how to become rich?
Algamish, amused, agreed.
And Arkad worked through the night, his back aching, and he finished it.
And then he held the old man to his promise.
And here is what Algamish told him.
This is the line.
This is the sentence I told you about at the very beginning.
Algamish said, I found the road to wealth when I decided that a part of all I earned was mine to keep.
And so should you.
A part of all you earn is yours to keep.
Now, when Arkad first heard it, he was confused.
He said, but all I earn is mine to keep, is it not?
And Algamish laughed at him, and explained the thing that most people go their whole lives without ever understanding.
He said no.
You pay the clothes maker.
You pay the sandal maker.
You pay for the food you eat.
You pay everyone else.
You work all month and then you hand every coin you earned to other people, and you keep nothing for yourself.
You fool, he said, you labor for everyone but yourself.
You might as well work for no pay at all.
Pay yourself first.
Take a portion of everything you earn, no less than one tenth, and before you pay a single other person, you set that tenth aside and you keep it.
That tenth is yours.
That is the part you keep.
Let me sit with you on this, because it sounds so simple that your brain wants to skip right past it, and I am begging you not to.
This is the foundation stone.
Everything else in this book is built on top of it.
The idea is this.
When your paycheck arrives, or your income comes in from whatever you do, the very first payment you make, before rent, before groceries, before the loan, before anything, is a payment to yourself.
At least ten percent.
You take it off the top and you put it away, and you live on the ninety percent that remains.
Most people do it backwards.
Most people pay everyone else all month long, and then whatever happens to be left over at the end, if anything is left over, that is what they save.
And the cruel truth is that there is never anything left over.
There is always one more thing to buy.
So they save nothing, year after year, no matter how much they earn.
The genius of pay yourself first is that it reverses the order.
You take your share before life gets a chance to take it from you.
And here is what will surprise you when you actually do it.
You will not miss it.
You will adjust to living on the ninety percent almost immediately, and meanwhile a little pile begins to grow that has never grown before in your life.
That is what Arkad did.
He began keeping one tenth of everything he earned.
And that brings us to his first great mistake, which is a lesson so important that Clason makes sure we do not miss it.
Arkad saved his tenth faithfully for a while, and then Algamish came back and asked him what he had done with it.

And Arkad proudly said, I gave it to Azmur the brickmaker, who was traveling over the far seas, and I told him to buy me the rare jewels of the Phoenicians, so that when he returned, we would sell them and make a great profit.
Algamish groaned.
He said, every fool must learn.
When Azmur returned, the so called jewels he had bought were worthless bits of glass.
The Phoenicians had cheated him.
Arkad had lost his entire savings.
Now why did this happen?
Algamish spelled it out, and this is one of the most valuable warnings in the entire book.
He said, you took the advice of a brickmaker about jewels.
Would you ask the baker about the stars?
Of course not.
A brickmaker knows bricks.
He knows nothing about precious stones.
Arkad took financial advice about jewels from a man whose only expertise was making bricks, and he paid for it with everything he had saved.
The lesson lands just as hard today as it did then.
Do not take financial advice from people who are not skilled in the specific thing you are asking about.
Your cousin who is great at his job but has never invested successfully is not the person to tell you where to put your retirement money.
The confident voice on the internet with no real track record in the thing they are selling is a brickmaker talking about jewels.
Advice is free, and it is worth exactly what you pay for it unless it comes from someone with genuine, proven skill in that exact field.
Arkad's savings were destroyed not because he failed to save, but because he handed his savings to the wrong advisor.
But he did not quit.
He saved again.
And this time, when he had money to invest, he sought counsel from men who actually knew about the thing at hand.
He gave his savings to Aggar the shield maker to buy bronze, because working with metals and trade was Aggar's genuine skill.
And it worked.
His money began to grow.
And Algamish, now old, told Arkad one more thing before the story of his youth ends.
He said, not only must you make your gold work for you, but you must make its children work, and its children's children, so that all of them may help to bring you the abundance you desire.
In other words, do not spend the profits your money earns.
Reinvest them.
Let the earnings themselves go back to work and earn more.
That is the ancient description of what we now call compounding, and it is the most powerful force in all of personal finance.
So Arkad rose.
He became rich.
And eventually, the King of Babylon himself noticed something troubling.
He noticed that in his great and wealthy city, only a handful of men knew how to acquire gold, while the vast majority of citizens had no idea how to keep any.
The wealth of Babylon was pooling in a few hands, and the king wanted to change that.
He wanted his whole city to be prosperous.
So he summoned Arkad and asked him a favor.
He said, will you teach your knowledge to the people?
Will you take a class of students and instruct them in the secrets of a fat purse, so that this wisdom spreads throughout Babylon?
And Arkad agreed.
And what he taught them has come down to us as the Seven Cures for a Lean Purse.
This is the heart of the book.
Seven remedies for the empty wallet.
Let us walk through every single one of them, slowly, because each one is a pillar you can build a life on.
The first cure is this.
Start thy purse to fattening.
This is the tenth again, but now it is the formal, foundational rule.
For every ten coins you place into your purse, take out only nine to spend.
Keep one.
Arkad told his students that when he first tried this, he expected to feel the loss, but he did not.
Life went on exactly as before, and yet his purse began to grow heavier.
He asked them, did you ever notice that the men who carry the fattest purses are usually the ones who spend the least?
And the men who spend everything have nothing?
Start thy purse to fattening.
Pay yourself the tenth, first, every time, without exception.
This is not the impressive cure.
It is the essential one.
Nothing else works without it.
The second cure is this.
Control thy expenditures.
Now, this is where Arkad addresses the objection that every single one of his students immediately raised, and that you are probably raising right now.
They said, how can we keep a tenth when what we earn is barely enough to cover what we need?
Our expenses already equal everything we make.
And Arkad gave them one of the most quietly devastating insights in the whole book.
He said, what each of us calls our necessary expenses will always grow to equal our incomes, unless we protest to the contrary.
Read that again in your mind.
What you call your necessary expenses will always grow to equal your income unless you fight back.
This is why the person earning fifty thousand a year is broke, and the person earning two hundred thousand a year is also broke.
As the money grows, the so called necessities grow right alongside it.
The bigger house.
The nicer car.
The upgraded everything.
It expands to swallow whatever comes in.
And Arkad's cure is to draw a hard line between your true necessary expenses and your mere desires.
He said, confuse not the necessary expenses with thy desires.
Every one of us has more desires than our income can satisfy.
That will always be true.

The task is to study your habits, budget carefully, and spend your ninety percent only on the things that give you the most value and the most life, while ruthlessly refusing the endless parade of desires that pretend to be needs.
Budget your expenditures so that your money covers your necessities and your worthwhile pleasures, without touching that guarded tenth.
The third cure is this.
Make thy gold multiply.
Once you have your growing pile of savings, that gold must not just sit there doing nothing.
Arkad said, a man's wealth is not in the coins he carries in his purse.
It is in the income he builds, the golden stream that flows continually into his purse whether he works or rests, whether he travels or stays home.
Put your savings to work so that they earn for you.
And then, crucially, put the earnings to work too.

Arkad tells the story of a man who lent out his money at interest, and every time the interest came back, he added it to the principal and lent it out again, so the sum grew larger and larger, and the earnings grew larger with it.
This is compounding again, and it is worth burning into your mind.
Your gold should labor for you.
And the children of your gold, the earnings, should also labor.
And their children too.
Over enough time, this turns a modest, steady saver into a wealthy person, not through luck, but through mathematics.
A stream of income that works while you sleep.
That is the real definition of wealth.
The fourth cure is this.
Guard thy treasures from loss.
This is the lesson Arkad learned so painfully from the jewels of Azmur.
He tells his students, the first sound principle of investment is security for thy principal.
Before you dream about how much a thing might earn you, ask a harder and more important question.
Can I get my money back?
Is the principal safe?
He warns them not to be seduced by the promise of large, unrealistic returns, because that is exactly the trap that captures the inexperienced.
He says, be not misled by thine own romantic desires to make wealth rapidly.
Study carefully, before parting with thy treasure, each assurance that it may be safely reclaimed.
And, echoing the brickmaker lesson, consult the wisdom of those experienced in the profitable handling of gold.
Guard your principal first.
A modest, safe return that protects your money will build wealth.
A spectacular return that risks losing everything will, sooner or later, lose everything.
It is far, far better to have a small return on your money than to lose the whole of it chasing a big one.
The fifth cure is this.
Make of thy dwelling a profitable investment.
Arkad encouraged the men of Babylon to own their own homes rather than paying rent forever into the pocket of a landlord.

He pointed out how much a family's life improves when they own the ground they live on, when the money that would have gone to rent instead goes toward owning something real, something that shelters the family and holds its value.
He said, owning his own home is a source of great satisfaction to a man, and it reduces his cost of living, freeing up more of his income for investment and enjoyment.
Now, in the modern world we can be a little more nuanced about this than Clason was.
Owning a home is not the right move for everyone in every situation, and there are seasons of life where renting makes better sense.
But the deeper principle underneath the cure is unshakable.
Do not pour your money endlessly into things that leave you with nothing to show for it.
Direct your resources, where you can, toward things you own, things that build equity, things that make your life more stable and your future more secure rather than simply enriching someone else.
The sixth cure is this.
Insure a future income.

Arkad reminded his students that no man lives forever, and that a wise man prepares for the days when he is old and can no longer work, and prepares also for his family in case something happens to him.
He said, provide in advance for the needs of thy growing age and the protection of thy family.
Plan ahead.
Set aside money now, steadily, patiently, so that when your working years are behind you, there is a fund to carry you, and so that if you are gone, your loved ones are not left with nothing.
This is the ancient argument for retirement saving and for life insurance and for building, brick by brick, a nest egg that will one day let your money do the working when your body no longer can.
It is not glamorous.
It is not exciting.
But the person who does this quietly for decades ends their life in dignity and security, while the person who ignores it ends it in fear and dependence.
And the seventh cure is this, and in some ways it is my favorite.
Increase thy ability to earn.
Arkad taught that the surest way to grow your wealth is to grow yourself.
He said, cultivate thy own powers, study and become wiser, become more skillful, and act so as to respect thyself.
Desire, he explained, must come first.
A person must genuinely want to become more, and want it specifically and strongly, not in some vague way.
The more skill you develop, the more knowledge you master, the more capable you become at your craft, the more you can earn.
And earning more, combined with all the other cures, accelerates everything.
He pointed to himself.
When he was a scribe carving tablets, he learned to do the work better and faster than others, and that skill is what opened the door to Algamish in the first place.
Invest in yourself.
Your ability to earn is an asset, perhaps the greatest asset you have when you are young, and unlike gold, no thief can steal it and no bad investment can destroy it.
Sharpen it constantly.
So those are the Seven Cures for a Lean Purse.
Start thy purse to fattening.
Control thy expenditures.
Make thy gold multiply.
Guard thy treasures from loss.
Make of thy dwelling a profitable investment.
Insure a future income.
And increase thy ability to earn.
Seven simple, ancient, unbreakable remedies.
If you did nothing else with the rest of this episode, if you took only those seven and lived by them, you would end up wealthier than the vast majority of people who ever hear them and forget them.
Now the book gives us another set of principles, and this one comes through one of my favorite stories, the story of Arkad's son.
Arkad had a son named Nomasir, and Arkad wanted to know whether his son could be trusted to keep and grow the family fortune, or whether he would squander it.
So he devised a test.

He gave Nomasir a bag of gold and a clay tablet on which were carved the Five Laws of Gold, and he sent the young man out into the world for ten years to make his own way, telling him to return and prove himself worthy of the inheritance.
And here is what is so human and so instructive about Nomasir's journey.
At first, he ignored the wisdom on the tablet.
He was young and confident.
He lost the entire bag of gold, all of it, in foolish ventures.
He bet on a horse race, he trusted a stranger who turned out to be a swindler, and soon he had nothing.
He was destitute.
And only then, humbled and desperate, did he finally sit down and actually study the Five Laws of Gold that his father had given him.
And once he began to live by those laws, everything changed.

He rebuilt his fortune, and he returned home after ten years not with one bag of gold but with wealth far greater than what his father had given him, along with a deep, earned understanding of how it all worked.
Arkad wept with joy, because his son had not just kept the gold, he had learned the laws that create gold, which is worth infinitely more.
So let us walk through those Five Laws of Gold, because they refine and deepen everything we have already talked about.
The first law of gold.
Gold comes gladly and in increasing quantity to any man who will put by not less than one tenth of his earnings to create an estate for his future and that of his family.
There it is again, the tenth, the foundation, now framed as the very first law.
Save at least a tenth, consistently, and gold will begin to come to you, gladly, and it will grow.
Everything starts here.
The second law of gold.
Gold laboreth diligently and contentedly for the wise owner who finds for it profitable employment, multiplying even as the flocks of the field.
Once you have gold, put it to work.
Gold that sits idle earns nothing.
Gold that is wisely invested labors for you tirelessly, day and night, and it multiplies.
Find profitable, sensible employment for your money and it will work harder for you than you ever could for yourself.
The third law of gold.
Gold clingeth to the protection of the cautious owner who invests it under the advice of men wise in its handling.
Notice the word clingeth.
Gold sticks to the careful person.
It stays with the one who is cautious, who does not gamble, and who seeks out the counsel of people who genuinely know how to handle money.
This is the brickmaker lesson elevated to a law.
When you invest under wise counsel and with caution, your gold stays with you and grows.
It clings to you.
The fourth law of gold.
Gold slippeth away from the man who invests it in businesses or purposes with which he is not familiar, or which are not approved by those skilled in its keeping.
Here is the flip side.
Gold slips away, it escapes, from the person who puts it into things he does not understand.
If you cannot clearly explain how an investment works and how it makes money, you have no business putting your gold into it.
Investing in what you do not understand is one of the surest ways to lose everything.
Nomasir lost his first bag exactly this way, trusting ventures he knew nothing about.
And the fifth law of gold.

Gold flees the man who would force it to impossible earnings, or who followeth the alluring advice of tricksters and schemers, or who trusts it to his own inexperience and romantic desires in investment.
Gold flees.
It runs away from the person who chases impossible returns, who falls for the smooth talk of con artists and get rich quick schemers, and who lets his own inexperience and wishful thinking guide him.
If a deal promises returns that sound too good to be true, they are too good to be true, and your gold will flee from you the moment you reach for them.

The tricksters and schemers of Babylon wore different clothes than the ones today, but they are the same people, offering the same impossible promises, and they are still separating the greedy and the naive from their savings.
Look at how those five laws mirror the seven cures, reinforcing them from a different angle.
Save a tenth.
Put it to work.
Be cautious and take wise counsel.
Never invest in what you do not understand.
And run from anything that promises impossible riches.
This is the entire philosophy of the book, told twice, once as remedies and once as laws, so that it sinks in deep.
Now the book gives us more stories, and each one carries its own lesson.
Let me tell you about Mathon, the gold lender, and his token box.

A young man named Rodan comes into a windfall, fifty pieces of gold given to him by the king, and immediately everyone in his life, especially his sister, is pressuring him to lend the money to her husband for a business scheme.
Rodan does not know what to do, so he goes to Mathon, an experienced gold lender, for advice.
And Mathon teaches him how to think about lending money and to whom.
Mathon keeps a token box, a chest full of little tokens and pledges left by everyone he has ever lent to, and each token has a story.
He tells Rodan that when he lends gold, he lends it based on the borrower's ability to repay and the safety of what backs the loan.
He warns that lending to a friend or family member out of pure kindness, when they have no real means to repay and no sound plan, is not kindness at all.
It often destroys both the money and the friendship.

Better, Mathon says, to help a person in ways that do not burden you with their whole risk, and to lend gold only where it is genuinely safe and where the borrower has demonstrated the capacity and character to return it.
Humble caution, he calls it.
If you would keep your gold safe, be careful to whom you entrust it, and demand security for it.
This is guard thy treasures from loss again, applied to the very human and very painful situation of being asked for a loan by someone you love.
Then there is the story I want to spend real time on, because it is the most emotionally powerful in the whole book, and it might be the most useful to you if you are carrying debt right now.
It is the story of Dabasir, the camel trader, and how he dug himself out of debt.
Dabasir was once a respectable man, but he fell into bad habits.
He spent freely, he borrowed to cover his spending, and his debts piled higher and higher until they crushed him.
He could not pay.
He fled the city to escape his creditors and fell in with a band of robbers, and through a series of disasters he ended up sold into slavery.
Picture that.
A free man, a tradesman, reduced to a slave because his debts spiraled out of control.
This is rock bottom.
And in his lowest moment, as a slave, the wife of his master says something to him that reaches him.
She asks him whether, in his soul, he is truly a free man or truly a slave.

She tells him that a man with the soul of a free man looks his troubles in the face and finds a way to repay what he owes and reclaim his honor, while a man with the soul of a slave whines and blames his fate and gives up.
And something in Dabasir wakes up.
He decides, right there, that he still has the soul of a free man.
He escapes across the desert, nearly dying of thirst and hunger, and he makes it back to Babylon with one burning purpose.
He is going to face every single one of his creditors and pay back every coin he owes.
And here is the plan he creates, and this is pure gold, so listen closely, especially if debt is weighing on you right now.
Dabasir sat down and did something almost no one in debt ever does.
He wrote down exactly who he owed, and how much, all of it, no hiding from the total.
And then he divided his income into three parts.
He decided that he would live on seventy percent of what he earned.
Seventy percent for all his needs, his food, his shelter, his life.
Then he would take twenty percent of everything he earned and divide it fairly among all his creditors, paying each of them a portion, steadily, month after month, until every debt was cleared.
And the final ten percent, he would keep for himself, because even while paying off debt, a man must still pay himself first and begin building something, or he will never break the cycle.
Think about how wise and how humane this is.
He did not try to throw every spare coin at his debt and leave himself nothing, because that path leads to despair and relapse.
He kept ten percent for himself so that his own pile could begin to grow even as he climbed out of the hole, which gave him hope and momentum.
He lived on seventy percent, disciplined but livable.
And he committed twenty percent, reliably, to his creditors.
And here is the beautiful part.
When he went to his creditors and explained this plan, most of them were glad, because a steady, honest partial payment is worth far more than a debtor who vanishes and pays nothing.
Some grumbled, but he paid them all the same, fairly, on schedule.

And over time, coin by coin, month by month, Dabasir paid off every last debt, restored his honor, and rebuilt his fortune, and he became a respected free man once again, in fact a wealthy camel trader.
The line Clason gives us from this story is one to tattoo on your heart.
Where the determination is, the way can be found.
Dabasir did not have some brilliant new income.
He had the same trade, the same city, the same coins.
What he found was determination, and once he had that, the way opened up in front of him.
If you are in debt right now, this is your blueprint.
Face the full number honestly.
Live on seventy percent.
Pay your creditors twenty percent steadily and fairly.
Keep ten percent for yourself so that you are building even as you are repaying.
And bring determination.
Where the determination is, the way can be found.
And there is a wonderful frame around this story, by the way.

Clason tells us that Dabasir carved this whole plan and this whole story onto clay tablets, and that thousands of years later, an archaeologist actually dug those tablets out of the ruins of Babylon and translated them, and a modern professor, deep in his own debt, read them and applied Dabasir's exact plan, seventy, twenty, ten, to his own life, and it worked, and it lifted him out of his debt too.
It is Clason's playful way of driving home the whole thesis of the book.
The laws do not age.
A plan carved in clay five thousand years ago still works for a man drowning in modern debt, because the math of money never changes.
Now let me give you two more pieces before we bring it home, because they add something the cures and laws do not quite capture on their own.
The first is the story of the Luckiest Man in Babylon, and the lesson about luck and action.
There is a debate among the men of Babylon about how to attract good luck.
Do you find it at the gaming tables?
At the races?
Through prayers to the gods?
And the wisdom they arrive at is this.
Good luck does not come from gambling, and it does not come from waiting around hoping.
Good luck follows opportunity.
Luck favors men of action.

When a genuine opportunity presents itself, the lucky man is the one who acts on it, promptly, decisively, while the unlucky man hesitates, delays, says he will get to it tomorrow, and lets the opportunity slip past him to someone else.
And the great villain of this lesson is a single word, and it is a word I want you to declare war on.
Procrastination.
Clason calls procrastination the thief of opportunity.

The tale is told of a man who had the chance to buy fertile land at a bargain because the seller needed money urgently, a golden opportunity right in front of him, and he delayed, he wanted to think it over, he wanted to wait, and by the time he made up his mind the opportunity was gone forever and someone else grew rich on that land.
The lesson is not that you should be reckless.
It is that when a sound opportunity is in front of you, one that fits everything you have learned about caution and safety, you must act, and not let delay steal it from you.
Men of action are favored by the goddess of good luck.
So when you know the right thing to do, do it now.
Do not let procrastination, the great thief, rob you of the good fortune that was meant to be yours.
And the second piece is the wall of Babylon, and the lesson of insurance and protection.

The great city of Babylon was defended by an enormous wall, so thick and so high that it was considered one of the wonders of the ancient world, and for generations it kept the city safe from every invader who came against it.

Clason tells the story of one such siege, of the old soldiers and citizens holding the wall through days of terrifying assault, until at last their own army returned and drove the enemy off, and the city was saved, because the wall held.
And the lesson he draws from that wall is protection.
We all need walls in our lives.
We need protection against the disasters that will come, because disasters do come, to everyone, sooner or later.
The wall is a picture of insurance, of savings, of the reserves that stand between you and ruin when the unexpected strikes.
A sudden illness.
A lost job.
An accident.
A death in the family.
The person with no wall is destroyed by the first serious blow that lands.
The person who has built walls, an emergency fund, insurance, savings set aside for the bad day, survives the siege and lives to prosper again.
We cannot live without adequate protection, Clason says.
So build your walls before you need them, because the day you need them is not the day you can build them.
Now let me pull one more thread, because there is a final story in the book that gives us the deepest lesson of all, and it is easy to miss.
It is the story of Sharru Nada, the merchant prince, and a young man named Hadan Gula.
Hadan Gula is the grandson of a man named Arad Gula, who had been Sharru Nada's dear friend and partner.

Hadan Gula is riding along with Sharru Nada, and he is a bit spoiled, wearing jewels, looking down slightly on the idea of hard work, expecting that wealth should simply come to a man of his standing.
And as they travel, Sharru Nada tells him a secret about his own past that the young man never knew.
Sharru Nada reveals that he himself was once a slave.

He tells the story of how, in his youth, he and Hadan Gula's grandfather, Arad Gula, were both enslaved, and how the two of them, through hard work, through the willingness to labor gladly and well, earned their way back to freedom and then to great fortune.
The secret Sharru Nada shares is the dignity and the power of work itself.
He says that work, done well and embraced rather than resented, was the friend that carried him from slavery all the way to becoming a merchant prince.
Work, he explains, is the finest friend a man can have.
Hard work, embraced with a good spirit, was the very thing that made everything else possible.
And that is a beautiful place to land, because it puts a floor under all the clever principles about tenths and investments and compounding.
None of it works without the willingness to do good, honest work, and to do it well, and to take pride in it rather than resenting it.
The saving, the investing, the budgeting, all of it rests on the foundation of a person who earns through diligent effort and treats their work as a friend rather than an enemy.
Bansir the chariot builder and Kobbi the musician worked hard, yes, but the missing piece for them was never their willingness to work.
It was that they never made their work pay them, because they never kept a part of what they earned.
Combine honest, embraced labor with the discipline of keeping a part of all you earn, and you have the complete formula.
The soul of a free man, and the laws of gold, working together.
So let us gather it all up, because I want you to walk away from this with something you can actually do, not just something you enjoyed hearing.
Here is the whole thing in one breath.
The laws of money have not changed in thousands of years.
A part of all you earn is yours to keep, so pay yourself first, at least one tenth, before anyone else.
Control your spending, and remember that your so called necessary expenses will grow to swallow your whole income unless you protest to the contrary.
Make your gold multiply by putting it to work and letting the earnings work too.
Guard your principal above all, and never chase impossible returns.
Own where you can rather than paying forever into someone else's pocket.
Provide for your future and your family in advance.
And relentlessly increase your ability to earn by growing your own skill.
Take advice only from people genuinely skilled in the matter at hand, never the brickmaker about jewels.
Invest only in what you understand.
Run from tricksters and schemes.
If you are in debt, live on seventy, pay creditors twenty, keep ten, and remember that where the determination is, the way can be found.
Act on opportunity, because procrastination is the thief.
Build your walls before the siege.
And treat your work as your friend.
Now, the concrete part.
Here is what I want you to actually do this week.
Not someday.
This week.

First, this week, open whatever account you keep your money in and set up an automatic transfer of ten percent of your income into a separate savings or investment account, timed to move the moment your income arrives, before you can spend it.
Make it automatic so your own willpower is not even part of the equation.
If ten percent feels impossible right now, start with five, or even three, but start, and then raise it.
The act of starting matters more than the amount.
Second, this week, sit down for twenty minutes and write out, honestly, where your money actually went last month.
Every category.
And then draw a clear line between your true necessary expenses and your desires that are pretending to be necessities.
Find one desire you have been calling a need, and cut it, and redirect that money to your tenth.
Just one, this week.
Third, if you are carrying debt, this week, write down every debt you have, the full ugly total, and sketch out Dabasir's plan.
What would it look like to live on seventy percent, put twenty toward your creditors, and keep ten for yourself?
Even if you cannot execute it perfectly yet, get the plan on paper, because where the determination is, the way can be found, and the plan is where determination becomes real.
Fourth, this week, identify one skill that would increase your ability to earn, one book, one course, one practice, and take the first small step on it.
Invest in the one asset no bad market and no thief can ever take from you, which is yourself.
And fifth, this week, notice one opportunity you have been procrastinating on, one sound thing you know you should act on, and act on it.
Kill the thief before it robs you again.
That is The Richest Man in Babylon.
A collection of little stories about an ancient city, dressed up in old robes, carrying the plainest and most reliable wealth wisdom ever written down.

Arkad was once a poor scribe carving clay tablets for almost nothing, and he became the richest man in the greatest city of the ancient world, and he did it with rules so simple that a child could understand them and so powerful that almost no one has the discipline to follow them.
You now know those rules.
The gap between knowing them and being wealthy is not intelligence, and it is not luck.
It is nothing more than the quiet, daily discipline of living by them.
A part of all you earn is yours to keep.
Start keeping it.
Starting this week.
